pair_rationale: Both objects belong to negotiation contexts; the unacceptable item targets the fact that break off selects talks or relations, not a proposal itself.
Both objects are legal-domain nouns; the unacceptable item keeps the domain but uses an event noun that cannot be handed over as custody or control.
Both outcomes are ranking positions; rejection depends on knowing that move up denotes upward rank movement.
Both nouns are transport-domain nouns; only a vehicle-like noun is compatible with the boarding sense.
Both nouns are writing-domain nouns; the unacceptable item changes the comparison set from same-class submissions to document parts.
Both complements are decision-domain nouns; the unacceptable item uses a state/result where an activity or commitment is expected.
Both objects are research-domain nouns; samples can be depleted as supplies, whereas findings are not normally used up as a stock.
Both objects are administrative/legal nouns; records can be surrendered to authorities, but permission is not an object surrendered in this frame.
Both objects belong to theater contexts; the unacceptable item uses a theater-related artifact rather than an event or performance.
Both nouns are sports-outcome nouns; lead can be regained, whereas loss is not normally regained by taking it back.
Both subjects are finance-domain nouns; investment can yield a benefit, while invoice is not normally an intransitive source of payoff.
Both nouns are transport-domain nouns; only the vehicle-like noun supports the leave-a-vehicle sense.
Both complements are legal-domain nouns; court is a location/event site, whereas testimony is not a place where someone turns up.
Both objects belong to talk-show discourse; the target sense selects an active speaker, not the topic of conversation.
Both objects are professional-service nouns; the target sense requires a person or role, not the service event itself.
Both nouns are document-domain nouns; the target sense selects an explanation or rules, not a document section as such.
Both nouns are sports-domain nouns; a play can be analyzed into parts, whereas a whistle is not an analytical problem in this frame.
Both complements are health-domain nouns; the target sense requires a person or group that can be contacted.
Both objects are sports-outcome nouns; a season can be transformed, whereas a defeat itself is not normally turned around after the fact.
Both nouns are research-discourse nouns; data can support a conclusion, but not confusion as a claim.
Both objects are finance/document nouns; mortgage fits the obtain-an-official-service sense, while receipt does not.
Both subjects are legal-domain nouns; the acceptable subject is hidden information, whereas the unacceptable subject is a person and risks alternate literal readings.
Both objects are policy/research nouns; reforms can cause change, whereas evidence is normally produced or found rather than brought about by reforms.
Both objects are research-domain nouns; analysis is a task that can be carried out, whereas hypothesis is a proposition to test.
Both objects are medical-domain nouns; infection can be excluded as a possibility, whereas symptoms are observed signs, not the ruled-out condition.
Both objects are academic-document nouns; the review can identify a limitation, whereas appendix is a document part and creates weaker target fit.
Both nouns are academic-document nouns; an abstract summarizes a study, not a heading.
Both complements are medical-domain nouns; the follow-up frame requires an event or case, not an instrument.
Both objects are document/information nouns; diagram can present a process clearly, not normally a footnote.
Both objects are classroom-domain nouns; equation is a problem that can be solved, whereas pencil is not.
